mainEntity.name: How much epoxy do I need for a garage floor?

acceptedAnswer.text: A standard 2-car garage (400-500 sq ft) needs about 1.5-2 gallons of thin-coat epoxy per coat, or 2 gallons of primer plus 2-3 gallons of color coat. Most kits cover 200-250 sq ft per kit. For a 2-car garage, plan on 2 kits.

mainEntity.name: What is the coverage rate for epoxy floor coating?

acceptedAnswer.text: Thin-coat epoxy covers 200-400 sq ft per gallon (depending on porosity). Self-leveling epoxy covers 12-16 sq ft per gallon at 1/8" thickness. Decorative flake broadcast coats cover 200-300 sq ft per gallon for the base coat.

mainEntity.name: Do I need primer for an epoxy floor?

acceptedAnswer.text: Primer is recommended for most floors, especially porous concrete. A primer coat improves adhesion and reduces pinholes. Primer covers 250-350 sq ft per gallon. On well-prepared, non-porous concrete, some systems allow skipping primer.

mainEntity.name: How many coats of epoxy do I need?

acceptedAnswer.text: Minimum 2 coats for most projects: 1 primer/base coat + 1 color coat. For high-traffic areas, add a clear topcoat (3 coats total). Self-leveling systems typically need 1 coat at 1/8" thickness. Decorative flake systems use 3 coats: primer, flake broadcast, and clear topcoat.

mainEntity.name: How much does epoxy flooring cost per square foot?

acceptedAnswer.text: DIY epoxy kits cost $1.50-$3.00 per sq ft for materials. Professional installation runs $3-$7 per sq ft for standard coatings and $7-$12 for decorative or self-leveling systems. A 2-car garage costs $600-$1,500 DIY or $1,500-$3,500 installed.

Self-leveling epoxy is a thick, pourable coating (1/8" to 1/4") that flows and levels itself for a smooth, seamless finish. It covers only 12-16 sq ft per gallon (much less than thin coats) but creates a durable, high-build floor. It is common in commercial kitchens, labs, and showrooms.

howItWorks:
How to Calculate Epoxy Floor Materials

Divide floor area by the coverage rate for your coating type, then multiply by number of coats. Add primer separately.

Gallons = (Area ÷ Coverage Rate) × Number of Coats
  • Thin coat: 200–400 sq ft/gal (use 300 as average)
  • Self-leveling: 12–16 sq ft/gal at 1/8" thick (use 14 avg)
  • Decorative flake: 200–300 sq ft/gal base (use 250 avg)
  • Primer: ~300 sq ft/gal, 1 coat recommended
  • Kits: typical kit covers ~250 sq ft
  • Porous concrete may reduce coverage by 20–30%
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

How much epoxy for a garage floor?

A 2-car garage (400-500 sq ft) needs 1.5-2 gallons of thin coat per coat, or 2 kits. Add primer at 1.5-2 gallons. Self-leveling needs 28-36 gallons for the same area.

What coverage rate per gallon?

Thin coat: 200-400 sqft/gal. Self-leveling: 12-16 sqft/gal. Flake systems: 200-300 sqft/gal for base coat.

Do I need primer?

Recommended for most floors, especially porous concrete. Primer covers 250-350 sqft/gal and improves adhesion and reduces pinholes.

How many coats of epoxy?

Minimum 2: primer + color coat. For high traffic, add a clear topcoat (3 total). Self-leveling usually needs just 1 thick coat.

How much does epoxy flooring cost?

DIY: $1.50-$3.00/sq ft for thin coat, $4-$8 for self-leveling. Pro install: $3-$12/sq ft. A 2-car garage runs $600-$1,500 DIY.

What is self-leveling epoxy?

A thick pourable coating (1/8"-1/4") that flows to a smooth finish. Covers only 12-16 sqft/gal but creates a very durable, seamless floor.

sections.title: What Is the Epoxy Floor Calculator?
sections.body: The Epoxy Floor Calculator is a free online tool designed for contractors and DIY builders who need quick, accurate calculations in the construction and building space. By entering your input mode, floor area, length, you get instant results including epoxy, kits, primer. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting epoxy right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In construction and building, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by contractors and DIY builders worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to plan your projects with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your input mode and need to find the right epoxy. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

faq.question: Is the Epoxy Floor Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard construction and building formulas trusted by contractors and DIY builders. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What input mode should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate input mode value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

faq.question: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, the Epoxy Floor Calculator is fully responsive and works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download needed.

faq.question: What does epoxy mean in my results?
faq.answer: The epoxy value represents the primary output of the calculation based on your inputs. Use it as a baseline figure for your construction and building planning and decision-making.
